title: [source] Group member expansion is recursive through nested groups, returns descendants r

given: Group A directly contains group B.
Group B directly contains asset X.
when: Members of A are requested with the default recursion setting.
then: The result includes B and X, keyed uniquely by their asset IDs.
The result does not add A merely because A was the query input.

why_non_obvious: `getGroupMembers` could plausibly mean immediate members only, or include the supplied groups. Its default recursion and replacement-based merge make it a transitive descendant lookup with unique IDs.

source_read.rule: Group member expansion is recursive through nested groups, returns descendants rather than the queried group itself, and deduplicates results by asset ID while preserving ID-keyed entities.

title: [source] Current group membership is a multi-valued snapshot from the single latest eligi

given: An asset has a completed group-assignment log at time 10 assigning it to groups A and B.
The asset has a later completed group-assignment log at time 20 with no group references.
when: Current groups are read at time 20 or later.
then: The asset belongs to no groups; A and B are not retained additively.

why_non_obvious: Names such as `getGroup` and “assignment” can suggest additive membership records. Instead, only one latest log is read, and its entire group-reference list is the current value (including an empty list).

source_read.rule: A group-assignment log may not make a group asset a member of itself or of any group that is already its recursive member as of the assignment timestamp.

title: [membership-logic] GroupMembershipInterface

given: an asset A and two groups G1, G2
when: A is assigned to G1, then re-assigned to G2
then: A is a member of G2 only; membership in G1 is revoked (latest-wins)

why_non_obvious: Membership is derived from the LATEST assignment event, not the set of all assignments — a reassignment REVOKES the prior membership. An additive "is there any assignment" read (the natural port) gets this wrong.

title: [field-computed] AssetGroupItemList

given: the underlying state the computed field derives from
then: the computed field reflects the change without a separate write

why_non_obvious: A computed field list derives its value from other state rather than storing it. A port that stores it as a plain field diverges when the source state changes.

title: [hook-implementation] ThemeHooks

given: the entity state the hook keys on
when: the lifecycle event that triggers the hook occurs
then: the hook's derived side effect is observable at the value boundary

why_non_obvious: A hook fires a side effect on an entity lifecycle event. A re-implementer who models only the primary write can miss the derived state the hook maintains.

title: [views-filter] QuantityViewsData

given: records that the filter would include and exclude
then: only the included records are returned, in the filter's order

why_non_obvious: A Views query/filter shapes what a read returns (visibility, ordering, exclusion). The filter semantics are invisible from the read's method name.
